slides.title: Discriminant of a Quadratic Equation
slides.html:
General Form of a Quadratic:

\[ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \]

Discriminant Formula:

\[ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac \]

Why is the discriminant important?

  • Tells us how many and what type of solutions the quadratic equation has.

Cases:

  • \(\Delta > 0\): Two distinct real solutions
  • \(\Delta = 0\): One real solution (a repeated root)
  • \(\Delta \lt 0\): No real solutions (complex roots)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Graphical Meaning of the Discriminant
slides.html:
How the Discriminant relates to the graph of \(y = ax^2 + bx + c\):

  • \(\Delta > 0\): The parabola intersects the \(x\)-axis at two points.
  • \(\Delta = 0\): The parabola is tangent to the \(x\)-axis (one point of contact).
  • \(\Delta \lt 0\): The parabola does not intersect the \(x\)-axis.

Implication: You can predict how many real solutions an equation has by simply analyzing its discriminant.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Number of Real Solutions
slides.html:
Strategy

Match coefficients of x and constants on both sides — they must be identical.

\[ 2x^2 - 13x + 21 = 0 \]

How many distinct real solutions does the given equation have?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Infinite solutions mean the two sides are the same line — all coefficients must match.
slides.strategy_hint: Match coefficients of x and constants on both sides — they must be identical.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Number of Real Solutions
slides.html:
We are given the quadratic equation:

\[ 2x^2 - 13x + 21 = 0 \]

Use the discriminant to determine the number of real solutions:

\[ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac = (-13)^2 - 4(2)(21) = 169 - 168 = 1 \]

Since \(\Delta > 0\), the equation has two distinct real solutions.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Discriminant and No Real Solutions
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Consider the quadratic equation:

\[ x^2 - 34x + c = 0 \]

In the given equation, \(c\) is a constant. The equation has no real solutions if \(c > n\).

What is the least possible value of \(n\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Using the Discriminant
slides.html:
A quadratic equation \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\) has:

  • Two real solutions if \(b^2 - 4ac > 0\)
  • One real solution if \(b^2 - 4ac = 0\)
  • No real solution if \(b^2 - 4ac \lt 0\)

In our equation:

\[ x^2 - 34x + c = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 1, \quad b = -34, \quad c = c \]

Compute the discriminant:

\[ \Delta = (-34)^2 - 4(1)(c) = 1156 - 4c \]

Set discriminant to be less than 0:

\[1156 - 4c \lt 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4c > 1156 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c > 289\]

Therefore, the equation has no real solutions when:

\[ c > 289 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{289}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: The Quadratic Formula
slides.html:
To solve any quadratic equation:

\[ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \]

Use the Quadratic Formula:

\[ x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} \]

Steps to Use the Formula:

  • Identify \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) from the equation.
  • Plug the values into the formula.
  • Simplify the square root (use the discriminant).
  • Solve for both values using \(+\) and \(-\).

Note: Always check the discriminant before solving to understand the type of roots.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Quadratic Equation with Radical Solutions
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Consider the equation:

\[ 2x^2 - 2 = 2x + 3 \]

Which of the following is a solution to the equation above?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Quadratic Equation with Radical Solutions
slides.html:
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Start by rearranging the equation:
    \[ 2x^2 - 2 = 2x + 3 \Rightarrow 2x^2 - 2x - 5 = 0 \]
  • Use the quadratic formula:
    \[ x = \frac{-(-2) \pm \sqrt{(-2)^2 - 4(2)(-5)}}{2(2)} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 40}}{4} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{44}}{4} \]
  • Therefore, the solutions are:
    \[ x = \frac{1 + \sqrt{11}}{2}, \quad x = \frac{1 - \sqrt{11}}{2} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{\frac{1 + \sqrt{11}}{2}}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Solving a Quadratic Equation Involving Radicals
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Solve the quadratic equation:

\[ x^2 - 2x - 9 = 0 \]

One solution to the equation can be written in the form \(1 + \sqrt{k}\), where \(k\) is a constant.

What is the value of \(k\)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving a Quadratic Equation Involving Radicals
slides.html:
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Use the quadratic formula:
    \[ x = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{(-2)^2 - 4(1)(-9)}}{2(1)} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 36}}{2} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{40}}{2} \]
  • Simplify:
    \[ x = \frac{2 \pm 2\sqrt{10}}{2} = 1 \pm \sqrt{10} \]
  • One solution is \(1 + \sqrt{10}\), so \(k = 10\).
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{10}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Smallest Solution to a Radical Equation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Solve the equation:

\[ \sqrt{(x - 2)^2} = \sqrt{3x + 34} \]

What is the smallest solution to the given equation?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving Radical Equation
slides.html:
Square both sides:

\[ (x - 2)^2 = 3x + 34 \Rightarrow x^2 - 4x + 4 = 3x + 34 \Rightarrow x^2 - 7x - 30 = 0 \Rightarrow (x - 10)(x + 3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 10 \quad \text{or} \quad x = -3 \]

Now check for extraneous solutions by plugging into the original equation:

  • For \(x = 10\):
\[ \sqrt{(10 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{3(10) + 34} \Rightarrow \sqrt{64} = \sqrt{64} \quad \text{valid} \]
  • For \(x = -3\):
\[ \sqrt{(-3 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{3(-3) + 34} \Rightarrow \sqrt{25} = \sqrt{25} \quad \text{valid} \]

Case 2: \(-(x - 2) = \sqrt{3x + 34}\)

This will yield the same roots already considered since squaring removes signs.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{-3}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Solving a Radical Equation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Solve the equation:

\[ \sqrt{2x + 6} + 4 = x + 3 \]

What is the solution set?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving Radical Equation
slides.html:
We start with the given equation:

\[ \sqrt{2x + 6} + 4 = x + 3 \]

Subtract 4 from both sides:

\[ \sqrt{2x + 6} = x - 1 \]

Now square both sides:

\[ 2x + 6 = (x - 1)^2 \Rightarrow 2x + 6 = x^2 - 2x + 1 \]

Move all terms to one side:

\[ 0 = x^2 - 4x - 5 \Rightarrow (x - 5)(x + 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 5 \quad \text{or} \quad x = -1 \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving a Radical Equation (Part 2)
slides.html:
Check for extraneous solutions:

For \(x = 5\):

\[ \sqrt{2(5) + 6} + 4 = \sqrt{16} + 4 = 4 + 4 = 8 \quad\text{and}\quad x + 3 = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \text{valid} \]

For \(x = -1\):

\[ \sqrt{2(-1) + 6} + 4 = \sqrt{4} + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 \quad\text{and}\quad -1 + 3 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \text{not valid} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Systems of Equations
slides.html:
03Up next

Systems of Equations

slides.title: What is a System of Equations?
slides.html:
• A system of equations is a set of two or more equations with the same variables.
  • The solution is the point(s) that make all equations true at the same time.

Example:

\[ \begin{cases} y = 2x + 1 y = -x + 4 \end{cases} \]

Find the value of \(x\) and \(y\) that satisfies both equations.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Solving by Graphing
slides.html:
• Plot both equations into the Desmos.
  • The intersection point is the solution to the system.
\[ \begin{cases} y = x^2 y = 2x + 3 \end{cases} \]

Graph to find the point(s) of intersection.

\[ \text{Solution: The two curves intersect at points where } x^2 = 2x + 3. \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Exactly One Real Solution
slides.html:
Given the system:

\[ \begin{cases} y = -1.5 y = x^2 + 8x + a \end{cases} \]

Question: If the system has exactly one real solution, what is the value of \(a\)?

Step 1: Set the two equations equal to each other:

\[ x^2 + 8x + a = -1.5 \Rightarrow x^2 + 8x + (a + 1.5) = 0 \]

Step 2: For a quadratic to have exactly one real solution, the discriminant must be zero:

\[ b^2 - 4ac = 0 \Rightarrow 8^2 - 4(1)(a + 1.5) = 0 \]

Step 3: Solve:

\[ 64 - 4(a + 1.5) = 0 \Rightarrow a + 1.5 = 16 \Rightarrow a = 14.5 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{14.5}\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Vieta's Theorem
slides.html:
04Up next

Vieta's Theorem

slides.title: Vieta's Theorem for Quadratic Equations
slides.html:
Given: A quadratic equation in standard form

\[ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \]

with roots \(r_1\) and \(r_2\).

Vieta's Theorem:

  • Sum of the roots: \(r_1 + r_2 = -\dfrac{b}{a}\)
  • Product of the roots: \(r_1 \cdot r_2 = \dfrac{c}{a}\)

Why is this useful?

  • Solve problems involving the relationships between roots without solving the equation.
  • Helps in constructing quadratics from given roots.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Sum of the Solutions of a Quadratic Equation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

What is the sum of the solutions to the given equation?

\[ x^2 - 40x - 10 = 0 \]

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Sum of the Solutions
slides.html:
We are given a quadratic equation:

\[ x^2 - 40x - 10 = 0 \]

For any quadratic equation of the form:

\[ ax^2 + bx + c = 0, \]

the sum of the solutions is given by the formula:

\[ \text{Sum} = -\frac{b}{a}. \]

Here, \(a = 1\), \(b = -40\). Thus,

\[ \text{Sum} = -\frac{-40}{1} = 40. \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Quadratic Product of Roots and Constants
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In the given equation:

\[ 57x^2 + (57b + a)x + ab = 0, \]

where \(a\) and \(b\) are positive constants, the product of the solutions is given by \(kab\), where \(k\) is a constant.

What is the value of \(k\)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Product of Solutions
slides.html:
We are given the quadratic:

\[ 57x^2 + (57b + a)x + ab = 0. \]

Using the quadratic formula properties, the product of the roots of

\[ Ax^2 + Bx + C = 0 \]

is:

\[ \frac{C}{A}. \]

So here:

\[ \text{Product of roots} = \frac{ab}{57}. \]

We are told this equals \(kab\), so:

\[ kab = \frac{ab}{57}. \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Application
slides.html:
05Up next

Application

slides.title: Application of Quadratic Functions
slides.html:
Real-World Example: Projectile Motion

The height \(h\) (in meters) of a ball thrown into the air after \(t\) seconds is modeled by a quadratic function:

\[ h(t) = -4.9t^2 + vt + h_0 \]

where:

  • \(-4.9\) is the acceleration due to gravity (in m/s\textsuperscript{2}),
  • \(v\) is the initial velocity (in m/s),
  • \(h_0\) is the initial height (in meters).

Key Features:

  • The maximum height occurs at the vertex.
  • The time to reach the ground is when \(h(t) = 0\) (solve the equation).

Application: Use quadratic equations to model and solve problems involving motion, area, and economics.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Understanding Intercepts in a Revenue Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The revenue \( f(x) \), in dollars, that a company receives from sales of a product is given by the function:

\[ f(x) = -500x^2 + 25,000x, \]

where \(x\) is the unit price, in dollars, of the product.

The graph of \( y = f(x) \) in the \(xy\)-plane intersects the \(x\)-axis at \(0\) and \(a\).

What does \(a\) represent?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Understanding Intercepts in a Revenue Function
slides.html:
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)

Explanation:

  • The function is a downward-opening parabola (\(a = -500 \lt 0\)).
  • The \(x\)-intercepts of the graph represent values of \(x\) for which \(f(x) = 0\), meaning revenue is $0.
  • At \(x = 0\), revenue is 0 — this makes sense because selling at $0 means no money is made.
  • At \(x = a\), the company again makes $0 revenue — likely because the price is too high and no one buys it.
  • Thus, \(a\) represents the price at which the revenue becomes zero again.
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Interpreting the Vertex of a Quadratic Function in Context
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function

\[ f(x) = \frac{1}{9}(x - 7)^2 + 3 \]

gives a metal ball's height above the ground \(f(x)\), in inches, \(x\) seconds after it started moving on a track, where \(0 \leq x \leq 10\).

Which of the following is the best interpretation of the vertex of the graph of \(y = f(x)\) in the \(xy\)-plane?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Vertex of a Quadratic in Real-World Context
slides.html:
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)

Explanation:

  • The function is written in vertex form: \(f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k\), where the vertex is at \((h, k)\).
  • Here, the vertex is at \((7, 3)\).
  • Since the coefficient of the squared term is positive \(\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)\), the parabola opens upward, and the vertex represents a minimum.
  • So, the ball reaches its minimum height of 3 inches 7 seconds after it starts moving.
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

Practice

slides.title: Question: Symmetry and Discriminant
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function \(f(x) = 3x^2 + bx + c\) is a quadratic function, where \(b\) and \(c\) are constants.

The graph of \(y = f(x)\) in the \(xy\)-plane does not intersect the \(x\)-axis. If \(f(-7) = f(9)\), which of the following could be the value of \(b + c\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Symmetry and Discriminant (Part 1)
slides.html:
We are given:

\[ f(x) = 3x^2 + bx + c, \quad f(-7) = f(9) \]

Use symmetry: \(f(-7) = f(9)\) implies that the axis of symmetry is the midpoint between \(-7\) and \(9\):

\[ x = \frac{-7 + 9}{2} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Vertex is at } x = 1 \]

The vertex of a quadratic \(f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c\) occurs at:

\[ x = -\frac{b}{2a} = -\frac{b}{6} \]

Set equal to 1:

\[ -\frac{b}{6} = 1 \Rightarrow b = -6 \]

Now plug \(b = -6\) into the original function:

\[ f(x) = 3x^2 - 6x + c \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Symmetry and Discriminant (Part 2)
slides.html:
Now compute \(b + c = -6 + c\), and check answer choices.

Try:

  • A) \(-1 \Rightarrow c = 5\)
  • B) \(-3 \Rightarrow c = 3\)
  • C) \(-5 \Rightarrow c = 1\)
  • D) \(-7 \Rightarrow c = -1\)

We also know the graph does not intersect the \(x\)-axis → discriminant \(\lt 0\):

\[ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac = (-6)^2 - 4(3)(c) = 36 - 12c \]

We want \(36 - 12c \lt 0 \Rightarrow c > 3\)

Only option that gives \(c > 3\) is:

Only \(c = 5\) satisfies \(c > 3\), giving \(b + c = -1\).

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Properties of a Quadratic Function (Practice)
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate each percentage change into a multiplier, then chain the operations.

The function is given as:

\[ f(x) = ax^2 + 4x + c \]

where \(a\) and \(c\) are constants. The parabola opens upward and has a vertex at point \((h, k)\). It is also given that:

Properties
Property\(k \lt 0\)
Property\(f(-9) = f(3)\)

Which of the following must be true?

  • I. \(c \lt 0\)
  • II. \(a \geq 1\)

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Word problem flow: define a variable → write the equation → solve → check the units.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate each percentage change into a multiplier, then chain the operations.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Use of Symmetry and Vertex
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

We are told:

\[ f(x) = ax^2 + 4x + c \quad \text{and} \quad f(-9) = f(3) \]

Step 1: Use symmetry of a parabola.

If \(f(-9) = f(3)\), then these points are symmetric about the vertex.

So, the axis of symmetry (i.e. x-value of vertex) is:

\[ x = \frac{-9 + 3}{2} = -3 \]

Step 2: Use vertex formula to confirm.

The x-coordinate of the vertex of a quadratic is given by:

\[ x = \frac{-b}{2a} \]

Here, \(f(x) = ax^2 + 4x + c\), so:

\[ \frac{-4}{2a} = -3 \Rightarrow a = \frac{2}{3} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Properties of a Quadratic Function (Practice) (Part 2)
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Thus, statement II: \(a \geq 1\) is not necessarily true.

Step 3: What about \(c \lt 0\)?

From the question, we know that \(k \lt 0\). Therefor, by using Vertex Formula

\[\frac{4ac-b^2}{4a}\lt 0\]

, we can get that \(c\lt 6\). Hence, Option II is wrong

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Parabola Vertex and Coefficient Sum
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In the \(xy\)-plane, a parabola has vertex \( (9, -14) \) and intersects the \(x\)-axis at two points.

If the equation of the parabola is written in the form:

\[ y = ax^2 + bx + c, \]

where \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are constants, which of the following could be the value of \(a + b + c\)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Convert from Vertex to Standard Form
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

We are given:

  • Vertex: \( (9, -14) \)
  • Parabola intersects the \(x\)-axis at two points \( \Rightarrow \) it opens upwards or downwards (not a single point).

Step 1: Use vertex form.

\[ y = a(x - 9)^2 - 14 \]

Step 2: Expand to standard form.

\[ y = a(x^2 - 18x + 81) - 14 = ax^2 - 18a x + 81a - 14 \]

So we identify:

\[ a = a, \quad b = -18a, \quad c = 81a - 14 \]

Step 3: Compute the sum \(a + b + c\):

\[ a + (-18a) + (81a - 14) = (a - 18a + 81a) - 14 = 64a - 14 \]

Since the vertex is below the \(x\)-axis and the parabola intersects the \(x\)-axis at two points, \(a > 0\). Testing \(a + b + c = -12\):

\[ 64a - 14 = -12 \Rightarrow a = \frac{1}{32} \]

This value works, so the answer is \(-12\).

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: System of Equations - One Point of Intersection
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Given the system of equations:

\[ y = 2x^2 - 21x + 64 \]
\[ y = 3x + a \]

In the system, \(a\) is a constant. The graphs intersect at exactly one point \((x, y)\) in the \(xy\)-plane. What is the value of \(x\)?

(Can you do this question without using Quadratic Formula ?)

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: System of Equations (Part 1)
slides.html:
We set the two expressions for \(y\) equal:

\[ 2x^2 - 21x + 64 = 3x + a \]

Move all terms to one side:

\[ 2x^2 - 24x + (64 - a) = 0 \]

Since the graphs intersect at exactly one point, this quadratic must have exactly one solution. That means the discriminant is zero:

\[ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 0 \]

Here, \(a = 2\), \(b = -24\), \(c = 64 - a\). So:

\[ (-24)^2 - 4(2)(64 - a) = 0 \]
\[ 576 - 8(64 - a) = 0 \Rightarrow 576 - 512 + 8a = 0 \Rightarrow 64 + 8a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -8 \]

Now substitute \(a = -8\) into the original system:

\[ y = 3x - 8 \quad \text{and} \quad y = 2x^2 - 21x + 64 \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: System of Equations (Part 2)
slides.html:
Set equal again:

\[ 2x^2 - 21x + 64 = 3x - 8 \Rightarrow 2x^2 - 24x + 72 = 0 \]

Divide by 2:

\[ x^2 - 12x + 36 = 0 \Rightarrow (x - 6)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{x = 6} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Finding the Value of a
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function

\[ f(x) = a(x - 3)(x - k) \]

is a quadratic, where \(a\) and \(k\) are constants.

The graph of \(y = f(x)\) in the \(xy\)-plane is a parabola with vertex \((5, -32)\). What is the value of \(a\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Finding the Value of a
slides.html:
Given: \(f(x) = a(x - 3)(x - k)\) and vertex is at \((5, -32)\).

Step 1: Use symmetry — the \(x\)-coordinate of the vertex is the midpoint of the roots:

\[ \frac{3 + k}{2} = 5 \Rightarrow 3 + k = 10 \Rightarrow k = 7 \]

So the function is:

\[ f(x) = a(x - 3)(x - 7) \]

Step 2: Plug in the vertex point \((5, -32)\):

\[ f(5) = a(5 - 3)(5 - 7) = a(2)(-2) = -4a \]
\[ -4a = -32 \Rightarrow a = 8 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Polynomial Functions
slides.html:
01Up next

Polynomial Functions

slides.title: Definition of Polynomial Functions
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition of Polynomial:

An expression of the form:

\[ a_nx^n + a_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1x + a_0 \]

where:

  • \(n\) is a nonnegative integer (called the degree).
  • \(a_n, a_{n-1}, \ldots, a_0\) are real numbers (called coefficients).

Terminology:

  • Monomial: A polynomial with one term (e.g., \(5x^3\))
  • Binomial: Two unlike terms (e.g., \(x^2 + 3x\))
  • Trinomial: Three unlike terms (e.g., \(x^2 + 3x + 2\))
  • Like Terms: Terms that have the same variable and exponent (e.g., \(3x^2\) and \(-2x^2\))
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Structure and Degree of Polynomials
slides.html:
Degree:

  • The degree of a monomial is the sum of the exponents of all variables in that term.
  • The degree of a polynomial is the greatest degree among all its terms.

Components of a Polynomial:

  • Leading Term: The term with the highest degree.
  • Leading Coefficient: The coefficient of the leading term.
  • Constant Term: A term with no variable (degree 0).

Example:

\[ f(x) = 3x^3 - 4x^2 + 7x + 18 \]
  • Degree: 3 \quad Leading Coefficient: 3 \quad Constant: 18
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Zeros of Polynomial Functions and Graph Behavior
slides.html:
Real Zeros of Polynomial Functions

When \(f\) is a polynomial function and \(a\) is a real number, the following are equivalent:

  • \(x = a\) is a zero of \(f\).
  • \(x = a\) is a solution of \(f(x) = 0\).
  • \((x - a)\) is a factor of \(f(x)\).
  • \((a, 0)\) is an \(x\)-intercept of the graph of \(f\).

Repeated Zeros and Multiplicity

  • A factor \((x - a)^k\), \(k > 1\), yields a repeated zero \(x = a\) of multiplicity \(k\):
    • If \(k\) is odd: the graph crosses the \(x\)-axis.
    • If \(k\) is even: the graph touches the \(x\)-axis.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Polynomial Matches the Graph
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The graph of a function \(f\) is shown. Which of the following functions could define \(f\)?

Choices:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Identifying a Polynomial from Its Graph
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Goal: Match the graph of \(f(x)\) to one of the given polynomial equations.

Step 1: Identify the Zeros

  • The graph crosses/touches the \(x\)-axis at:
    \[ x = -3,\quad x = -1,\quad x = 2 \]

Step 2: Analyze Multiplicities

  • Touches at \(x = -1\) and \(x = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) even multiplicity (likely squared).
  • Crosses at \(x = -3\) \(\Rightarrow\) odd multiplicity (likely linear).

Step 3: Determine Equation

\[ f(x) = (x + 3)(x + 1)^2(x - 2)^2 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Solving a Factored Equation with Radicals
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

\[ \frac{5}{8}(5x + 8)\left(x + \sqrt{5k + 8}\right)\left(x - \sqrt{5k + 8}\right) = 0 \]

In the given equation, \(k\) is a positive constant. The product of the solutions to the equation is \(64\). What is the value of \(k\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving a Factored Equation with Radicals
slides.html:
We are given:

\[ \frac{5}{8}(5x + 8)(x + \sqrt{5k + 8})(x - \sqrt{5k + 8}) = 0 \]

The three roots come from:

  • \(5x + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -\frac{8}{5}\)
  • \(x + \sqrt{5k + 8} = 0 \Rightarrow x = -\sqrt{5k + 8}\)
  • \(x - \sqrt{5k + 8} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \sqrt{5k + 8}\)

So the roots are:

\[ x = -\frac{8}{5},\quad -\sqrt{5k + 8},\quad \sqrt{5k + 8} \]

Let’s compute the product of the roots (since the coefficient \(\frac{5}{8}\) does not affect the roots):

\[ \frac{8}{5}(5k + 8) = 64 \Rightarrow (5k + 8) = \frac{64 \cdot 5}{8} = 40 \Rightarrow 5k = 32 \Rightarrow k = 6.4 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{6.4}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Polynomial Long Division
slides.html:
Purpose:

Polynomial long division is used to divide a polynomial by another polynomial of lower or equal degree.

Steps:

  • Divide the leading term of the dividend by the leading term of the divisor.
  • Multiply the divisor by the result and subtract from the dividend.
  • Repeat the process with the remainder.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Polynomial Long Division
slides.html:
Example:

\[ \frac{2x^3 + 3x^2 - 5x + 6}{x - 2} \]
  • Divide \(2x^3 \div x = 2x^2\)
  • Multiply: \(2x^2(x - 2) = 2x^3 - 4x^2\)
  • Subtract: \((2x^3 + 3x^2) - (2x^3 - 4x^2) = 7x^2\)
  • Continue until remainder

Result:

\[ \frac{2x^3 + 3x^2 - 5x + 6}{x - 2} = 2x^2 + 7x + 9 + \frac{24}{x - 2} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Remainder Theorem
slides.html:
Theorem Statement:

If a polynomial \(f(x)\) is divided by \((x - c)\), then the remainder is:

\[ \text{Remainder} = f(c) \]

Why this works:

According to the Division Algorithm:

\[ f(x) = (x - c)q(x) + r \]

where:

  • \(q(x)\) is the quotient
  • \(r\) is the remainder

Substitute \(x = c\):

\[ f(c) = (c - c)q(c) + r = 0 + r = r \Rightarrow \boxed{f(c) = \text{Remainder}} \]

Key Use:

Quickly evaluate whether \((x - c)\) is a factor of \(f(x)\) — if \(f(c) = 0\), then \((x - c)\) is a factor!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Polynomial Divisibility
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The polynomials are defined as:

\[ f(x) = 2x^3 + 6x^2 + 4x, \quad g(x) = x^2 + 3x + 2 \]

Which of the following polynomials is divisible by \( 2x + 3 \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Polynomial Divisibility
slides.html:
To test divisibility by \( 2x + 3 \), substitute \( x = -\frac{3}{2} \). If the expression evaluates to 0, the polynomial is divisible by \( 2x + 3 \).

Try option B:

\[ p(x) = f(x) + 3g(x) \Rightarrow p\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right) = \frac{3}{4} + 3 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{3}{4} = 0 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Remainder Theorem and Polynomial Evaluation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

For a polynomial \( p(x) \), the value of \( p(3) = -2 \). Which of the following must be true about \( p(x) \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Remainder Theorem and Polynomial Evaluation
slides.html:
We are told that \( p(3) = -2 \). According to the Remainder Theorem, the remainder when a polynomial \( p(x) \) is divided by \( x - a \) is \( p(a) \).

  • Since \( p(3) = -2 \), the remainder when dividing \( p(x) \) by \( x - 3 \) is \(-2\).
  • This directly matches option D.
  • Options A, B, and C all refer to whether a linear binomial is a factor of \( p(x) \), which would require the remainder to be 0 at that root, but we are only given a non-zero value at \( x = 3 \).
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Rational Functions
slides.html:
02Up next

Rational Functions

slides.title: Introduction to Rational Functions
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A rational function is a quotient of two polynomial functions, written in the form:

\[ f(x) = \frac{N(x)}{D(x)} \]

where:

  • \(N(x)\) and \(D(x)\) are polynomials,
  • \(D(x) \ne 0\).

Domain:

The domain includes all real numbers except those for which \(D(x) = 0\) (division by zero is undefined).
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Question: Behavior of Rational Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function is defined as:

\[ p(x) = \frac{2x - 1}{(x - 4)^2 - 6(x - 4) + 9} \]

For what value of \( x \) can the function \( p(x) \) not cross the \( x \)-axis?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Behavior of Rational Function
slides.html:
To determine when \( p(x) \) cannot cross the \( x \)-axis, we need to find when the numerator \( 2x - 1 = 0 \) has no solution due to the denominator restricting the domain.

But since the function crosses the \( x \)-axis when \( p(x) = 0 \), this only happens when the numerator is zero and the denominator is non-zero.

So, we look for values of \( x \) where the denominator is undefined or causes a vertical asymptote, because these are values where the function can never cross the \( x \)-axis.

Denominator:

\[ (x - 4)^2 - 6(x - 4) + 9 \]

Let \( u = x - 4 \), then:

\[ u^2 - 6u + 9 = (u - 3)^2 \Rightarrow (x - 7)^2 \]

So denominator becomes zero when:

\[ x - 7 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 7 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{7}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Undefined Values of Rational Functions
slides.html:
Key Concept:

A rational function is undefined at values of \(x\) that make the denominator equal to zero.

General Form:

\[ f(x) = \frac{N(x)}{D(x)} \]
  • \(N(x)\) is the numerator
  • \(D(x)\) is the denominator
  • The function is undefined where \(D(x) = 0\)

Example:

\[ f(x) = \frac{4}{x - 5} \]
  • Set denominator equal to zero: \(x - 5 = 0\)
  • Solution: \(x = 5\)
  • Therefore, \(f(x)\) is undefined at \(x = 5\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.html:
03Up next

Practice

slides.title: Question: Intercepts of a Quadratic Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The graph of \(y = f(x)\) has a \(y\)-intercept at \((0, 12)\) and \(x\)-intercepts at \((-3, 0)\) and \((2, 0)\).

Which of the following equations could define \(f\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Intercepts of a Quadratic Function
slides.html:
We are given:

  • \(x\)-intercepts at \(x = -3\) and \(x = 2\), so the factored form must contain \((x + 3)\) and \((x - 2)\).
  • \(y\)-intercept is at \((0, 12)\).

Try Option A:

\[ f(x) = (x + 3)^2(x - 2) \]

At \(x = 0\):

\[ f(0) = (0 + 3)^2(0 - 2) = 9 \cdot (-2) = -18 \ne 12 \]

Try Option B:

\[ f(x) = (x + 3)(x - 2)^2 \]

At \(x = 0\):

\[ f(0) = (0 + 3)(0 - 2)^2 = 3 \cdot 4 = 12 \]

Correct!

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Distinct Zeros of a Polynomial
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

\(f(x) = -x(x - a)(x + a)(x + b)(x + b)\)

In the given function \(f\), \(a\) and \(b\) are positive constants and \(a \ne b\).

For how many distinct values of \(x\) does \(f(x) = 0\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Distinct Zeros of a Polynomial
slides.html:
We are solving for the number of distinct values of \(x\) such that:

\[ f(x) = -x(x - a)(x + a)(x + b)(x + b) \]

Set each factor equal to zero:

\[ x = 0,\ x = a,\ x = -a,\ x = -b \]

(Note: \(x + b\) appears twice, but it yields the same solution \(x = -b\).)

Since \(a \ne b\) and both are positive, all four roots are distinct.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Behavior of Rational Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function is defined as:

\[ p(x) = \frac{3x + 2}{(x - 1)^2 - 4(x - 1) + 4} \]

For what value of \(x\) can the function \(p(x)\) not cross the \(x\)-axis?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Behavior of Rational Function
slides.html:
We are given:

\[ p(x) = \frac{3x + 2}{(x - 1)^2 - 4(x - 1) + 4} \]

Thus, \(p(x)\) cannot cross the \(x\)-axis at:

\[ \boxed{x = 3} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Polynomial Evaluation and Remainder Theorem
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

For a polynomial \(q(x)\), the value of \(q(4) = 7\).

Which of the following must be true about \(q(x)\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Polynomial Evaluation and Remainder Theorem
slides.html:
Given that \(q(4) = 7\), by the Remainder Theorem, we know that:

\[ \text{The remainder when } q(x) \text{ is divided by } x - 4 \text{ is } q(4) = 7. \]

So the correct statement is:

\[ \boxed{\text{C) The remainder when } q(x) \text{ is divided by } x - 4 \text{ is } 7.} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Definition of Exponential Function
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Up next

Definition of Exponential Function
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Definition of Exponential Function
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Exponential Function: A function of the form:

\[ f(x) = a^x \]

where:

  • \(a > 0\)
  • \(a \ne 1\)
  • \(x\) is any real number
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Key Properties of Exponential Functions
slides.html:
If \(f(x) = a^x\), then:

Properties
PropertyDomain: \(\mathbb{R}\) (all real numbers)
PropertyRange: \((0, \infty)\)
Property\(f(0) = 1\) (since \(a^0 = 1\))
PropertyContinuous and smooth curve
PropertyHorizontal asymptote at \(y = 0\)

Growth vs Decay:

  • If \(a > 1\), function exhibits exponential growth
  • If \(0 \lt a \lt 1\), function exhibits exponential decay
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Identifying k in an Exponential Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

For the exponential function \(f\), the value of \(f(1)\) is \(k\), where \(k\) is a constant.

Which of the following equivalent forms of the function \(f\) shows the value of \(k\) as the coefficient or the base?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
We are told \(f(1) = k\). Let's plug \(x = 1\) into each option and see which form gives \(f(1) = k\) as the coefficient or base.

  • A. \(f(1) = 50(2)^2 = 50 \cdot 4 = 200\)
  • B. \(f(1) = 80(2)^1 = 160\)
  • C. \(f(1) = 128(2)^0 = 128 \cdot 1 = 128\)
  • D. \(f(1) = 205(2)^{-1} = \frac{205}{2} = 102.5\)

Only in option C, we get \(f(1) = 128\), and the coefficient is \(k\) directly.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Exponential Growth Relationship
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Two variables, \(x\) and \(y\), are related such that for each increase of \(1\) in the value of \(x\), the value of \(y\) increases by a factor of \(4\). When \(x = 0\), \(y = 200\). Which equation represents this relationship?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Exponential Growth Relationship
slides.html:
This is an exponential relationship where \(y\) increases by a factor of \(4\) for every \(1\) increase in \(x\). The general exponential form is:

\[ y = a \cdot b^x \]

Given:

  • Initial value: \(y = 200\) when \(x = 0\)
  • Growth factor: \(b = 4\)

So the equation becomes:

\[ y = 200(4)^x \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Finding Constant in Exponential Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function \(f\) is defined by \(f(x) = a^x + b\), where \(a\) and \(b\) are constants. In the \(xy\)-plane, the graph of \(y = f(x)\) has:

  • an \(x\)-intercept at \((2, 0)\)
  • a \(y\)-intercept at \((0, -323)\)

What is the value of \(b\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Finding Constant in Exponential Function
slides.html:
We are given two points:

  • \(f(2) = 0 \Rightarrow a^2 + b = 0\)
  • \(f(0) = -323 \Rightarrow a^0 + b = -323\)

From the second equation:

\[ 1 + b = -323 \Rightarrow b = -324 \]

Substitute \(b = -324\) into the first equation:

\[ a^2 - 324 = 0 \Rightarrow a^2 = 324 \Rightarrow a = 18 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{-324}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

Graphs and Transformation

slides.title: Graphs of Exponential Functions
slides.html:
Graphs of \( y = a^x \)

In the same coordinate plane, sketch the graph of each function.

  • \( f(x) = 2^x \)
  • \( g(x) = 4^x \)

Begin by constructing a table of values.

Observation:

  • Exponential functions increase/decrease rapidly
  • All graphs pass through the point \((0, 1)\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Transformations of the Exponential Function
Transformations of the Exponential Function
slides.html:
• a. \( g(x) = 3^{x+1} \) \\ Since \( g(x) = 3^{x+1} = f(x + 1) \), the graph of \( g \) is a horizontal shift left by 1 unit.
  • b. \( h(x) = 3^x - 2 \) \\ Since \( h(x) = 3^x - 2 = f(x) - 2 \), the graph of \( h \) is a vertical shift downward by 2 units.
  • c. \( k(x) = -3^x \) \\ Since \( k(x) = -3^x = -f(x) \), the graph of \( k \) is a reflection across the \( x \)-axis.
  • d. \( j(x) = 3^{-x} \) \\ Since \( j(x) = 3^{-x} = f(-x) \), the graph of \( j \) is a reflection across the \( y \)-axis.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: X-Intercepts of an Exponential Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Given the equation:

\[ y = 9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x + c} - b, \]

how many times does the graph of the given equation in the \(xy\)-plane cross the \(x\)-axis, where \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are positive constants such that \(a > 6\) and \(b > c\)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: X-Intercepts of an Exponential Function
slides.html:
We are given the exponential function:

\[ y = 9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x + c} - b \]
  • Since \(a > 6\), \(\frac{a}{6} > 1\), meaning the function is increasing.
  • The expression \(9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x + c}\) is always positive.
  • As \(x \to -\infty\), \(\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x + c} \to 0\), and \(y \to -b\), so the horizontal asymptote is \(y = -b\).
  • As \(x\) increases, \(y\) increases beyond \(-b\).
  • Since \(y = 0\) when \(9\left(\frac{a}{6}\right)^{x + c} = b\), and this equation has exactly one solution for \(x\), the graph crosses the \(x\)-axis exactly once.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Application
slides.html:
03Up next

Application

slides.title: Applications of Exponential Functions
slides.html:
Exponential functions are used to model real-world situations like:

  • Population Growth: \(P(t) = P_0 \cdot a^t\)
  • Compound Interest: \(A = P(1 + r)^t\)
  • Radioactive Decay: \(M(t) = M_0 \cdot a^{-t}\)
  • Bacterial Growth

Example: A population doubles every 3 years:

\[ P(t) = 100 \cdot 2^{t/3} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Exponential Function with Time Units — Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The given function \(p(t) = 90,\!000(1.06)^t\) models the population of Lowell \(t\) years after a census.

Which of the following functions best models the population of Lowell \(m\) months after the census?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Exponential Function in Terms of Different Units
slides.html:
The original exponential model is:

\[ p(t) = 90,000(1.06)^t \]

where \(t\) is measured in years.

To express the model in terms of months, we must use:

\[ t = \frac{m}{12} \]

Substitute into the original function:

\[ r(m) = 90,000(1.06)^{\frac{m}{12}} \]

This gives us the population model in terms of months.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
\[ \boxed{r(m) = 90,000(1.06)^{\frac{m}{12}}} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Exponential Modeling — Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function \(P\) models the population, in thousands, of a certain city \(t\) years after 2005:

\[ P(t) = 290(1.04)^{\left( \frac{4}{6} \right)t} \]

According to the model, the population is predicted to increase by \(n\%\) every 18 months.

What is the value of \(n\)?

Choices:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Exponential Modeling — Solution
slides.html:
Given:

\[ P(t) = 290(1.04)^{\left( \frac{4}{6} \right)t} \]

Step 1: Convert 18 months to years

\[ 18 \text{ months} = \frac{18}{12} = 1.5 \text{ years} \]

Step 2: Plug in \(t = 1.5\) to evaluate the growth over 18 months

\[ P(1.5) = 290(1.04)^{\left( \frac{4}{6} \right)(1.5)} = 290(1.04)^1 \]

Conclusion: The population grows by a factor of \(1.04\) every 18 months, or 4%.

Answer
C \(n = 4\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: The function \[ f(t) = 20(1 + 15100 )^t \] models…
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function

\[ f(t) = 20\left(1 + \frac{15}{100} \right)^t \]

models the temperature, in degrees Celsius, of a metal alloy used in an experiment, where \(t\) is the number of seconds after the experiment began.

Which of the following is the best interpretation of the number \(15\) in this context?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: X-Intercepts of an Exponential Function
slides.html:
The function is:

\[ f(t) = 20\left(1 + \frac{15}{100} \right)^t = 20(1.15)^t \]

This is an exponential growth function of the form:

\[ f(t) = a(1 + r)^t \]

Where \(a = 20\) is the initial value, and \(r = 0.15\) or \(15\%\) is the percent increase per second.

Thus, the number \(15\) represents the percent increase per second in temperature.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: The population of trees in a forest has been…
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The population of trees in a forest has been decreasing by 6 percent every 4 years. The population at the beginning of 2015 was estimated to be 14,000.

If \(P\) represents the population of trees in the forest \(t\) years after 2015, which of the following equations best defines \(P\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The population of trees in a forest has been…
slides.html:
We are told the population decreases by 6% every 4 years.

This is exponential decay, so the decay factor is:

\[ 1 - 0.06 = 0.94 \]

Every 4 years, the population is multiplied by \(0.94\). Since \(t\) is in years, and \(0.94\) applies every 4 years, the exponent must be \(\frac{t}{4}\).

So the equation is:

\[ P = 14,000(0.94)^{\frac{t}{4}} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

Choose your answer

slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The amount of data, in gigabytes, stored in a database…

slides.title: Question: The exponential function g is defined by the given…
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The exponential function \(g\) is defined by the given equation:

\[ g(x) = 256(0.61)^{\frac{x}{30}} \]

The equation can be rewritten as:

\[ g(x) = 256\left(1 - \frac{k}{100}\right)^{\frac{x}{10}}, \]

where \(k\) is a constant.

What is the value of \(k\), to the nearest whole number?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The exponential function g is defined by the given…
slides.html:
We are given:

\[ g(x) = 256(0.61)^{\frac{x}{30}} = 256\left(1 - \frac{k}{100}\right)^{\frac{x}{10}} \]

Let:

\[ r = 1 - \frac{k}{100} \Rightarrow \left(1 - \frac{k}{100}\right)^{\frac{x}{10}} = (0.61)^{\frac{x}{30}} \]

So:

\[ r^{\frac{x}{10}} = (0.61)^{\frac{x}{30}} \Rightarrow r^{1/10} = (0.61)^{1/30} \]

Raise both sides to the 10th power:

\[ r = \left((0.61)^{1/30}\right)^{10} = (0.61)^{\frac{1}{3}} \]

Now compute \((0.61)^{1/3} \approx 0.850\) (cube root of 0.61)

So:

\[ 1 - \frac{k}{100} \approx 0.85 \Rightarrow \frac{k}{100} \approx 0.15 \Rightarrow k \approx 15 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: The function \[ W(t) = 830(1.09)^68t \] gives the…
slides.html:

The function

\[ W(t) = 830(1.09)^{\frac{6}{8}t} \]

gives the value, in dollars, of a certain investment after \(t\) years.

If the value of the investment increases by 9% every \(n\) months, what is the value of \(n\)?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The exponential function g is defined by the given…

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{n = 16}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: To examine how a certain virus spreads, scientists…
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

To examine how a certain virus spreads, scientists introduced the virus to cells in a test tube and found that the number of infected cells in the test tube grew exponentially over time.

The given function \(C\) models the number of infected cells in the test tube \(t\) days after the virus was introduced:

\[ C(t) = 100(2)^{\frac{t}{5}} \]

Which is the best interpretation of \((2)^{\frac{t}{5}}\) in this context?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: To examine how a certain virus spreads, scientists…
slides.html:
The function is:

\[ C(t) = 100(2)^{\frac{t}{5}} \]

This is an exponential growth function with base \(2\) and exponent \(\frac{t}{5}\), which means the quantity doubles every 5 days. That’s because:

\[ C(5) = 100(2)^{1} = 200 \Rightarrow \text{doubling in 5 days} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: The function f is defined for all x 0. Which…
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The function \(f\) is defined for all \(x \geq 0\).

Which of the following equivalent forms of the function \(f\) displays, as a constant or coefficient, the minimum value of \(f\), where \(x \geq 0\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The function f is defined for all x 0. Which…
slides.html:
We are asked to identify the expression that makes the minimum value of \(f(x)\) appear as a constant or coefficient, given that \(x \geq 0\).

For an exponential function in the form:

\[ f(x) = A \cdot B^{x + c} \]

The minimum occurs when the exponent is minimized (i.e., \(x = 0\)).

Let’s test \(x = 0\) in all options:

A) \(f(0) = 50(2.5)(1.25)^{-2} = 125 \cdot (1/1.5625) \approx 80\)

B) \(f(0) = 125(0.8)(1.25)^{-1} = 100 \cdot (1/1.25) = 80\)

C) \(f(0) = 64(1.25)^1 = 80\)

D) \(f(0) = 80(0.64)(1.25)^2 = 80(0.64)(1.5625) = 80\)

All give the same result for \(x = 0\): \(f(0) = 80\). But the key is: which one displays the minimum value directly as a constant or coefficient?

Only option D has:

\[ f(x) = \boxed{80}(0.64)(1.25)^{x + 2} \]

and no exponent adjustment on the 80 — it’s clearly the base value when \(x = 0\).

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Ratio
slides.html:
01Up next

Ratio

slides.title: What is a Ratio?
slides.html:
A ratio is a comparison between two numbers. It can be written in three different ways:

\[ \frac{a}{b}, \quad a:b, \quad \text{or } a \text{ to } b \]

Example: The ratio of boys to girls in a class is 3 to 4.

  • This means for every 3 boys, there are 4 girls.
  • The fraction form is \( \frac{3}{4} \).
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Example: Growth Factor Calculation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Species of treeGrowth factor
River birch3.5
Cottonwood2.0
Black walnut4.5
White birch5.0
American elm4.0
Pin oak3.0
Shagbark hickory7.5

One method of calculating the approximate age, in years, of a tree of a particular species is to multiply the diameter of the tree, in inches, by a constant called the growth factor for that species.

The table on the previous slide provides the growth factors for eight species of trees.

If a white birch tree and a pin oak tree each now have a diameter of 1 foot (12 inches), which of the following will be closest to the difference in inches of their diameters 10 years from now?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Watch the method, then close the solution and redo the problem from scratch.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Example: Solution
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Step 1: Calculate the diameter increase for each tree in 10 years

\(Age = Diameter \times Growth\ Factor \)

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{1.3}\)

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Watch the method, then close the solution and redo the problem from scratch.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Up next Rates and Units
slides.html:
02Up next

Rates and Units

slides.title: Rates and Unit Rates
slides.html:
A rate is a special type of ratio that compares different units.

Example: A car travels 180 miles in 3 hours. The rate is:

\[ \frac{180 \text{ miles}}{3 \text{ hours}} = 60 \text{ miles per hour} \]

A unit rate simplifies the rate so that the denominator is 1.

Example: If 12 apples cost $6, the unit price per apple is:

\[ \frac{6 \text{ dollars}}{12 \text{ apples}} = 0.50 \text{ dollars per apple} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Unit Conversion
slides.html:
Key Idea: Multiply by a conversion factor equal to 1.

\[ \text{Quantity in old unit} \times \frac{\text{new unit}}{\text{old unit}} = \text{Quantity in new unit} \]

Example 1: Inches to Feet

\[ 36 \text{ inches} \times \frac{\frac{1}{12} \text{ foot}}{{} \text{ 1 inches}} = 3 \text{ feet} \]

Example 2: Minutes to Seconds

\[ 5 \text{ minutes} \times \frac{60 \text{ seconds}}{1 \text{ minute}} = 300 \text{ seconds} \]

Tips:

  • Always write units clearly.
  • Cancel the units you don’t need.
  • Use a conversion factor that equals 1.
  • Check your final units!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Unit Conversion (Square Miles to Square Yards)
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A certain town has an area of 4.36 square miles. What is the area, in square yards, of this town?

Given: \(1 \text{ mile} = 1,760 \text{ yards}\)

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Unit Conversion
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1: Understanding the conversion factor

We know that:

\[ 1 \text{ mile} = 1,760 \text{ yards} \]

Since we are dealing with square miles, we square both sides:

\[ 1 \text{ square mile} = (1,760 \text{ yards})^2 = 3,097,600 \text{ square yards} \]

Step 2: Multiply by given area

The given area is \( 4.36 \) square miles. Converting to square yards:

\[ 4.36 \times 3,097,600 = 13,505,536 \text{ square yards} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Proportions
slides.html:
03Up next

Proportions

slides.title: Solving Proportions
slides.html:
A proportion is an equation stating that two ratios are equal:

\[ \frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d} \]

Cross Multiplication: If \( \frac{3}{4} = \frac{x}{12} \), solve for \( x \):

\[ 3 \times 12 = 4 \times x \]
\[ 36 = 4x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 9 \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Solving for n
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Problem Statement:

If

\[ \frac{4a}{b} = 6.7 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{a}{bn} = 26.8, \]

what is the value of \( n \)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving for n
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1: Express \( a/b \) in terms of known values

From the first equation:

\[ \frac{4a}{b} = 6.7 \]

Dividing both sides by 4:

\[ \frac{a}{b} = \frac{6.7}{4} = 1.675 \]

Step 2: Substitute into the second equation

\[ \frac{a}{bn} = 26.8 \]

Rewriting using \( \frac{a}{b} = 1.675 \):

\[ \frac{1.675}{n} = 26.8 \]
Final Answer
\[ \boxed{0.0625} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.html:

Question 1: If the ratio of boys to girls in a school is 5:8, and there are 200 boys, how many girls are there?

Question 2: A car travels 240 miles in 4 hours. What is its speed in miles per hour?

slides.title: Conclusion
slides.html:
• Ratios compare two quantities
  • Rates involve different units, and unit rates have a denominator of 1
  • Proportions are equations that show two equal ratios
  • Use cross multiplication to solve proportions
  • These concepts are frequently tested on the SAT!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Ratio Word Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Find the rate of change first (Δy/Δx), then build the linear model.

The ratio of blueberries to grapes in a fruit salad is \(5\) to \(12\). If there are \(x\) blueberries in the fruit salad, which expression represents the number of grapes in the fruit salad?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Word problem flow: define a variable → write the equation → solve → check the units.
slides.strategy_hint: Find the rate of change first (Δy/Δx), then build the linear model.

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
To maintain the ratio \(5:12\) (blueberries to grapes), we set up the proportion:

\[ \dfrac{5}{12} = \dfrac{x}{g} \Rightarrow g = \dfrac{12}{5}x \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B} \)\(\dfrac{12}{5}x\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Unit Conversion: Area
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The area of Wompatuck State Park in Massachusetts is \(5.5\) square miles. Which of the following is closest to the area of this park, in square kilometers? (Use \(1\) kilometer \(= 0.62\) mile.)

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
We are given:

\[ \text{Area in square miles} = 5.5, \quad 1 \text{ kilometer} = 0.62 \text{ mile} \]

So,

\[ 1 \text{ square mile} = \left(\dfrac{1}{0.62}\right)^2 \approx 2.6 \text{ square kilometers} \]
\[ 5.5 \text{ square miles} \approx 5.5 \times 2.6 = 14.3 \text{ square kilometers} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D} \)\(14.3\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
The scale is \(5\) inches in the model : \(16\) feet in reality.

Let the actual width be \(10w\) feet. To find the width in the model, apply the ratio:

\[ \frac{5}{16} = \frac{\text{model width}}{10w} \Rightarrow \text{model width} = \frac{5}{16} \cdot 10w = \frac{50}{16}w = \frac{25}{8}w \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B} \)\(\dfrac{25}{8}w\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?
