slides.title: Up next Introduction to Percent
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Up next

Introduction to Percent
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Introduction to Percent
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: Percent means "per hundred." It represents a ratio of a number to 100.

\[ \text{Percent} = \frac{\text{Part}}{\text{Whole}} \times 100 \]

Example: 25 out of 100 is written as 25%.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Percent Change Formula
slides.html:
Formula:

\[ \text{Percent Change} = \frac{\text{New Value} - \text{Original Value}}{\text{Original Value}} \times 100 \]

Example: If a price increases from $80 to $90:

\[ \frac{90 - 80}{80} \times 100 = 12.5\% \]

Key Concept: Positive percent change indicates an increase, negative indicates a decrease.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Percent Greater or Less Than
slides.html:
01Up next

Percent Greater or Less Than

slides.title: Percent Greater or Less Than
slides.html:
Translates to...Example
\(p%\) of \(x\)\(\dfrac{p}{100} \times x\)\(15%\) of \(x \Rightarrow \dfrac{15}{100} \times x = 0.15x\)
\(p%\) greater than \(x\)\(\left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100} \right)x\)\(15%\) greater than \(x \Rightarrow \left(1 + \dfrac{15}{100} \right)x = 1.15x\)
\(p%\) less than \(x\)\(\left(1 - \dfrac{p}{100} \right)x\)\(15%\) less than \(x \Rightarrow \left(1 - \dfrac{15}{100} \right)x = 0.85x\)

Example: If 200 is x% greater than 50, what is x?

\[ 50 \times \left(1 + \frac{x}{100}\right) = 200 \]

Key Takeaway: If something is 50% greater than a value, multiply by 1.5.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Percent Relationship Between a, b, and c
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The number \( a \) is 70% less than the positive number \( b \).

The number \( c \) is 60% greater than \( a \).

The number \( c \) is how many times \( b \)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Percent Relationship Between a, b, and c
slides.html:
• Since \( a \) is 70% less than \( b \), we express it as:
    \[ a = (1 - 0.7)b = 0.3b \]
  • Since \( c \) is 60% greater than \( a \), we express it as:
    \[ c = (1 + 0.6)a = 1.6a \]
  • Substituting \( a = 0.3b \):
    \[ c = 1.6(0.3b) = 0.48b \]
  • Thus, the number \( c \) is \(\mathbf{0.48}\) times \( b \).
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{0.48}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Key Takeaways
slides.html:
• Percent represents "per hundred."
  • Use the percent change formula to calculate increases and decreases.
  • "Percent greater than" and "percent less than" refer to the original value.
  • Use division to find the original value when given the increased price.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Store Cost from Sale Price
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The regular price of a shirt at a store is \($11.70\).

The sale price of the shirt is 80% less than the regular price,

and the sale price is 30% greater than the store's cost for the shirt.

What was the store's cost, in dollars, for the shirt?

(Disregard the $ sign when entering your answer.)

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Store Cost from Sale Price
slides.html:
• Step 1: Find the sale price of the shirt
    \[ \text{Sale Price} = (1 - 0.80) \times 11.70 = 0.20 \times 11.70 = 2.34 \]
  • Step 2: Set up equation for the store’s cost
    \[ \text{Sale Price} = (1 + 0.30) \times \text{Cost} \]
    \[ 2.34 = 1.30 \times \text{Cost} \]
  • Step 3: Solve for Cost
    \[ \text{Cost} = \frac{2.34}{1.30} = 1.80 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{1.80}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Increased By vs. Decreased By
slides.html:
02Up next

Increased By vs. Decreased By

slides.title: ``Increased By'' vs. ``Decreased By''
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definitions:

  • Increased by \(p\%\): Multiply by \(\left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)\)
  • Decreased by \(p\%\): Multiply by \(\left(1 - \frac{p}{100}\right)\)

Examples:

  • \(120\) increased by \(25\%\):
    \[ 120 \times \left(1 + \frac{25}{100}\right) = 120 \times 1.25 = 150 \]
  • \(150\) decreased by \(20\%\):
    \[ 150 \times \left(1 - \frac{20}{100}\right) = 150 \times 0.8 = 120 \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Important Notes
slides.html:
Important Notes:

  • Be careful: A 20% decrease followed by a 20% increase does not return to the original value!
    \[ 100 \to 80 \to 96 \]
  • Always treat ``increased by'' and ``decreased by'' as multiplicative — not additive.
  • If you are given a final value and percentage, reverse the multiplier to solve for the original.

Key Insight: Think in terms of scaling, not just adding or subtracting!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Net Percentage Increase
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The value of a collectible comic book increased by \(167\%\) from the end of 2011 to the end of 2012, and then decreased by \(16\%\) from the end of 2012 to the end of 2013.

What was the net percentage increase in the value of the collectible comic book from the end of 2011 to the end of 2013?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Net Percentage Increase
slides.html:
Let the original value at the end of 2011 be \(100\) (for simplicity).

  • After a \(167\%\) increase:
    \[ 100 + 1.67 \times 100 = 267 \]
  • After a \(16\%\) decrease:
    \[ 267 \times (1 - 0.16) = 267 \times 0.84 = 224.28 \]
  • Net increase from original \(100\):
    \[ 224.28 - 100 = 124.28 \]
    The net increase is \(124.28\%\).
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Practice
slides.html:
03Up next

Practice

slides.title: Question: Percent Greater Relationship
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In 2010, the number of houses built in Town A was \(n\) percent greater than the number of houses built in Town B. If \(700\) houses were built in Town A in 2010, which expression represents the number of houses built in Town B in 2010?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Percent Greater Relationship
slides.html:
Let \(x\) be the number of houses in Town B.

Town A had \(n\%\) more than Town B, so:

\[ 700 = x \left( \dfrac{100 + n}{100} \right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 700 \cdot \left( \dfrac{100}{100 + n} \right) \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Percent of c that is a
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The sum of positive numbers \(a\) and \(b\) is \(4.76\) times the positive number \(c\), and \(a\) is \(124\%\) of \(b\). What percent of \(c\) is \(a\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Percent of c that is a
slides.html:
We are given:

\[ a + b = 4.76c \quad \text{and} \quad a = 1.24b \]

Substitute \(a\) into the sum:

\[ 1.24b + b = 4.76c \Rightarrow 2.24b = 4.76c \Rightarrow b = \dfrac{4.76c}{2.24} \]

Now substitute \(b\) into \(a = 1.24b\):

\[ a = 1.24 \cdot \dfrac{4.76c}{2.24} = \dfrac{1.24 \cdot 4.76}{2.24}c = 2.635c \]

Convert to percent:

\[ a = 263.5\% \text{ of } c \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Percent of z that is x
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The sum of positive numbers \(x\) and \(y\) is \(3.5\) times the positive number \(z\), and \(x\) is \(160\%\) of \(y\). What percent of \(z\) is \(x\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

PresenterJack ZengFromNovel Prep

slides.title: Lesson outline
slides.html:
Lesson outline

SAT Unit 3.3

One-Variable Data

36 slides · tap any section to jump there instantly.
slides.study_tip: Use this outline to jump straight to any section — great for review or picking up where you left off.

slides.title: Up next Mean, Median, and Mode
slides.html:
01Up next

Mean, Median, and Mode

slides.title: Understanding Mean, Median, and Mode
slides.html:
Mean (Average):

\[ \text{Mean} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n} \]
  • Effect of Outliers: Extremely sensitive to outliers.
  • Interpretation: Represents a balance point of the data.
  • If one value increases by \(k\), the mean increases by \(\frac{k}{n}\).

Median (Middle Value):

  • The value separating the higher half from the lower half.
  • Robustness: Resistant to outliers; a better measure of central tendency for skewed data.
  • A single extreme value has no effect unless it shifts the order.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: More on Mean and Median with Data Behavior
slides.html:
When is Median Preferred over Mean?

  • Skewed data (e.g., income distribution).
  • Presence of outliers or extreme values.

Examples of Change Impact:

  • Add 1000 to an income of \(30,000\): Mean jumps; median may stay same.
  • Remove a high outlier: Mean drops; median may remain unchanged.

Mode:

  • The value(s) that appear most frequently in a data set.
  • May have none, one, or multiple modes.
  • Useful for categorical or discrete data.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Data Set and Mean Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Data set \( A \) consists of 10 positive integers less than 60. The list shown gives 9 of the integers from data set \( A \):

\[ 43,\ 45,\ 44,\ 43,\ 38,\ 39,\ 40,\ 46,\ 40 \]

The mean of these 9 integers is 42. If the mean of data set \( A \) is an integer that is greater than 42, what is the value of the largest integer from data set \( A \)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Data Set and Mean Problem
slides.html:
Let the missing number be \( x \). The sum of the 9 known numbers is:

\[ 43 + 45 + 44 + 43 + 38 + 39 + 40 + 46 + 40 = 378 \]

Mean of all 10 numbers must be an integer greater than 42. So:

\[ \frac{378 + x}{10} > 42 \Rightarrow 378 + x > 420 \Rightarrow x > 42 \]

Since the mean must also be an integer, try smallest integer value for the mean greater than 42, which is 43:

\[ \frac{378 + x}{10} = 43 \Rightarrow 378 + x = 430 \Rightarrow x = 52 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{52}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Effect of Adding an Outlier to a Data Set
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Data set F consists of 55 integers between 170 and 290. Data set G consists of all the integers in data set F as well as the integer 10.

Which of the following must be less for data set F than for data set G?

  • I. The mean
  • II. The median

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Effect of Adding an Outlier to a Data Set
slides.html:
• Adding an extreme low value (like 10) to data set F will significantly reduce the mean of the data set, since the mean is sensitive to outliers.
  • However, the median is the middle value. Since 10 is far below the range of values in set F (170–290), it will simply be placed at the beginning and not affect the central value of the sorted list.

Conclusion:

  • Mean of F \(>\) Mean of G
  • Median remains unchanged
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Standard Deviation and Range
slides.html:
02Up next

Standard Deviation and Range

slides.title: Standard Deviation: Definition and Analysis
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

\[ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2} \]
  • Measures the spread or dispersion of data around the mean.
  • A small \(\sigma\) indicates data points are close to the mean.
  • A large \(\sigma\) implies data is more spread out.

How It Changes:

  • Adding a constant to all values: \(\sigma\) stays the same.
  • Multiplying all values by a constant \(k\): \(\sigma\) becomes \(|k|\sigma\).
  • Introducing an outlier: \(\sigma\) increases significantly.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Range: Definition and Behavior
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

\[ \text{Range} = \text{Maximum value} - \text{Minimum value} \]

Properties:

  • Measures the total spread of a data set.
  • Only uses the two extreme values; does not reflect the distribution of other data points.
  • Sensitive to outliers — a single large or small value can drastically change the range.

How Range is Affected:

  • Adding a constant to all values: No change in range.
  • Multiplying all values by a positive constant \(k\): Range becomes \(k \cdot \text{(original range)}\).
  • Replacing max or min with a more extreme value increases the range.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Modified Data Set and Statistical Measures
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A data set of 27 different numbers has a mean of 33 and a median of 33. A new data set is created by adding 7 to each number in the original data set that is greater than the median and subtracting 7 from each number in the original data set that is less than the median.

Which of the following measures does NOT have the same value in both the original and new data sets?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
• The median stays the same, since the middle number (33) is unchanged.
  • The mean unchanged.
  • The sum of the values changes for the same reason the mean does — because of the increases and decreases.
  • The standard deviation increases, since numbers are being moved further from the center (more spread).

The only measure that does not stay the same is the standard deviation.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.html:
Up next

Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Dot Plot
slides.html: A dot plot is a simple way to display numerical data using dots. Each dot represents one occurrence of a data value, and the dots are stacked vertically above a number line corresponding to the data values.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Histogram
slides.html: A Histogram is a type of bar graph that represents the frequency distribution of a dataset. It displays data in intervals (also called bins), where the height of each bar corresponds to the frequency of data within that interval. Unlike bar charts, there are no gaps between the bars in a histogram.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Box Plot
slides.html:
A box plot, also known as a box-and-whisker plot, graphically represents the five-number summary:

\[ \text{Minimum}, \quad Q_1 \ (\text{First Quartile}), \quad \text{Median}, \quad Q_3 \ (\text{Third Quartile}), \quad \text{Maximum} \]
slides.study_tip: Quick recap — make sure you can recall each bullet without looking.

slides.title: Contingency Table (Two-Way Table)
slides.html:
A Contingency Table or Two-Way Table summarizes the frequency of occurrences for two categorical variables. It helps analyze the relationship between these variables by organizing data into rows and columns.

The table below shows the relationship between students' Gender and their Preference for Study Subject.

\hline GenderMathScienceHistoryTotal
Male20251560
Female30204090
Total504555150
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Box Plot Interpretation – Mass Comparison
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The box plots summarize the masses, in kilograms, of two groups of gazelles. Based on the box plots, which of the following statements must be true?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
• The median of group 1 is clearly to the right of the median of group 2.
  • Since median values are shown as vertical lines inside each box, it is evident that:
    \[ \text{Median}_{\text{Group 1}} > \text{Median}_{\text{Group 2}}. \]
  • Statements about the mean cannot be verified from a box plot alone.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Dot Plot and Data Transformation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The dot plot represents the 15 values in data set A. Data set B is created by adding \( 56 \) to each of the values in data set A.

Which of the following correctly compares the medians and the ranges of data sets A and B?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation
slides.html:
• Adding the same number to all values in a data set increases the median by that amount.
  • However, the spread (range) remains unchanged because both the maximum and minimum values are increased equally.
  • Thus:
    • Median of B is greater than median of A
    • Range of B is equal to range of A
Correct Answer: \( \boxed{C} \)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Median Comparison – Grouped Data
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

For a certain computer game, individuals receive an integer score that ranges from 2 through 10. The table below shows the frequency distribution of the scores of the 9 players in group A and the 11 players in group B.

ScoreGroup AGroup B
210
310
420
514
632
700
802
911
1002
Total911

Question: The median of the scores for group B is how much greater than the median of the scores for group A?

Your answer

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Median Comparison – Grouped Data
slides.html:
To find the medians:

  • Group A (9 players): Median is the 5\textsuperscript{th} value. Sorted values based on frequency:
    \[ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 4,\ \boxed{5},\ 6,\ 6,\ 6,\ 9 \]
    Median of Group A = \( \boxed{5} \)
  • Group B (11 players): Median is the 6\textsuperscript{th} value. Sorted values based on frequency:
    \[ 5,\ 5,\ 5,\ 5,\ 6,\ \boxed{6},\ 8,\ 8,\ 9,\ 10,\ 10 \]
    Median of Group B = \( \boxed{6} \)
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{1}\)

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Median Comparison of Data Sets P and Q

Walk through the solution

Step 1. Find the median of data set P:

Data set P (ordered): 41, 43, 45, 45, 47, 47, 51, 51, 55

Median: 47

Step 2. Find the median of data set Q:

Data set Q (ordered): 33, 33, 36, 36, 38, 38, 40, 40, 40

Median: 38

Step 3. Difference:

\(47 - 38 = 9\)

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{9}\)

If an item with a price of 25 dollars is removed from the shopping cart, what will be the effect on the mean and median price of the items in the shopping cart?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The frequency table summarizes the prices of 30 items…
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Mean Analysis:

Removing a low-priced item (25) from the dataset will increase the overall mean, because the total sum decreases by less than proportionally compared to the decrease in count.

Step 2. Median Analysis:

Since there are 30 items initially, the median is the average of the 15th and 16th values when ordered. After removing one item priced at 25 (the lowest), there are now 29 items. The new median is the 15th value, which remains at 55.

Conclusion:

The mean will increase, and the median will remain the same.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)

slides.title: Question: Comparing Frequency Tables for Data Set A
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Data set A

ValueFrequency
3011
5011
7011

The frequency table shown represents data set A. Which of the following frequency tables represents a data set that has a mean greater than the mean of data set A?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Comparing Frequency Tables for Data Set A
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Mean of data set A:

\[ \text{Mean} = \frac{(30 \cdot 11) + (50 \cdot 11) + (70 \cdot 11)}{33} = \frac{330 + 550 + 770}{33} = \frac{1650}{33} = 50. \]

Step 2. Compare each option:

  • A: All frequencies are equal, mean = 50 (same as set A).
  • B: More weight on 70 and less on 30, so mean > 50.
  • C: More weight on 30, mean < 50.
  • D: Equal frequencies on 30 and 70 but same as set A on 50; mean = 50.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Comparing Data Sets A and B
slides.html:

Data set AData set B
ValueFrequencyValueFrequency

The tables show the frequencies of the values for data sets A and B. Which of the following statements best compares the means and the standard deviations of data sets A and B?

slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Comparing Data Sets A and B
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Compare the means:

  • Data set B is a translation of data set A by adding 3 to each value.
  • Therefore, the mean of data set B is 3 more than the mean of data set A.

Step 2. Compare the standard deviations:

  • Adding a constant shifts all data points equally, so standard deviation does not change.
  • Therefore, the standard deviation of data set B is equal to that of data set A.

Conclusion:

  • The mean of data set B is greater than the mean of data set A.
  • The standard deviations of data sets A and B are equal.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)

slides.title: Up next Linear vs Exponential Model
slides.html:
01Up next

Linear vs Exponential Model

slides.title: Linear Models
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A linear model shows a constant rate of change — the dependent variable increases or decreases by the same amount for every unit increase in the independent variable.

\[ y = mx + b \]

Where:

  • \(m\) is the slope (rate of change)
  • \(b\) is the \(y\)-intercept (starting value)

Key Characteristics:

  • Graph is a straight line.
  • Change is additive.
  • Example: Earning $15/hour → \(y = 15x\)
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Exponential Models
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

An exponential model shows a constant percentage rate of change — the quantity increases or decreases by the same proportion over equal intervals.

\[ y = a \cdot b^x \]

Where:

  • \(a\) is the initial value
  • \(b\) is the growth (\(b > 1\)) or decay (\(0 \lt b \lt 1\)) factor

Key Characteristics:

  • Graph is curved — increasing/decreasing rapidly.
  • Change is multiplicative.
  • Example: Population doubles each hour → \(y = 100 \cdot 2^x\)
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Exponential Decay – Interpretation of Parameters
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question:

The function \( M(t) = 4000(0.6)^{\frac{t}{3}} \) models the mass, in nanograms, of a particle after \( t \) years.

Which of the following is the best interpretation of \( (0.6)^{\frac{t}{3}} \) in this context?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Decay Rate over Time
slides.html:
Explanation:

The exponential function is of the form:

\[ M(t) = 4000 \cdot (0.6)^{\frac{t}{3}}, \]

which means that every 3 years, the mass is multiplied by 0.6 — a 40% decrease from the previous amount.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Comparing Linear vs Exponential Models
slides.html:
Linear: Constant difference

Exponential: Constant ratio

FeatureLinearExponential
General Form\(y = mx + b\)\(y = a \cdot b^x\)
Rate of ChangeConstant (add/subtract)Constant (multiply/divide)
Graph ShapeStraight lineCurve (growth/decay)
Example$10 per dayDouble each day
Real-World UseSalaries, feesPopulations, investments

Key Insight:

If the rate of change is constant difference, it's linear.

If the rate of change is constant ratio, it's exponential.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Linear Functions – Percentage Relationship
slides.html:
Since \( 201\% \) of \( x \) means \( f(x) = 2.01x \), this is a linear function with a positive slope, so it increases at a constant rate.

Correct Answer

Time (hours)Population

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Identify the Model Type
slides.html:
The population doubles each hour: \(100 \rightarrow 200 \rightarrow 400 \rightarrow 800\). This shows a constant ratio (multiply by 2), so the model is \( P(t) = 100 \cdot 2^t \).

slides.title: Up next Scatterplots and Regression
slides.html:
02Up next

Scatterplots and Regression

slides.title: Understanding Scatterplots
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A scatterplot is a graph that displays pairs of data points \((x, y)\) as dots in the coordinate plane.

Purpose:

  • Visualizes the relationship between two numerical variables.
  • Reveals trends, clusters, outliers, or correlation direction.

Types of Trends:

  • Positive association: As \(x\) increases, \(y\) increases.
  • Negative association: As \(x\) increases, \(y\) decreases.
  • No association: No visible pattern or relationship.

Note: Scatterplots do not imply causation, only correlation.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Scatterplots and Linear Regression
slides.html: Linear regression is a line of best fit through a scatterplot: \( y = mx + b \), where \( m \) is slope and \( b \) is the \( y \)-intercept. It minimizes squared vertical distances (residuals) and is used for prediction. The closer the points are to the line, the stronger the linear relationship.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Line of Best Fit from Scatterplot
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The scatterplot above shows a company's ice cream sales \( d \), in dollars, and the high temperature \( t \), in degrees Celsius (°C), on 12 different days. A line of best fit for the data is also shown.

Which of the following could be an equation of the line of best fit?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Line of Best Fit from Scatterplot
slides.html:
From the scatterplot:

  • The line of best fit passes through approximately \( (12, 480) \) and \( (24, 876) \)
  • Estimating the slope:
    \[ \text{slope} = \frac{876 - 480}{24 - 12} = \frac{396}{12} = 33 \]
  • Plug into point-slope form:
    \[ d - 480 = 33(t - 12) \Rightarrow d = 33t + 84 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Slope of Line of Best Fit
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Which of the following is closest to the slope of the line of best fit shown?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Which of the following is closest to the slope of the…
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Find two points on the line of best fit:

  • At \(x=1\), \(y \approx 17\).
  • At \(x=4\), \(y \approx 13\).

Step 2. Compute the slope:

\[ \text{slope} = \frac{13-17}{4 - 1} = \frac{-4}{3} \approx -1.33. \]

Step 3. Choose the closest answer:

\[ -1.33 \text{ is closest to } -1.38. \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

\[ 0.12 \times 8,400 = 1,008. \]

slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Since shelves are stocked at a constant rate, the rate of decrease in the number of shelves left to stock is constant. That means \(p(t)\) is decreasing linearly.

Correct Answer

slides.title: Question: For the function f, the value of f(x) is equal…

slides.title: Answer Explanation: For the function f, the value of f(x) is equal…
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. The function is \(f(x) = \frac{1}{2}x\).

Step 2. Since the function is a constant multiple of \(x\), it is a linear function.

Step 3. The coefficient \(\frac{1}{2}\) is positive, so the function is increasing.

\(\boxed{C}\)

slides.title: Up next Probability
slides.html:
01Up next

Probability

slides.title: Definition of Probability
slides.html:
Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Probability is a measure of how likely an event is to occur. It is defined as:

\[ P(\text{Event}) = \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of possible outcomes}} \]

Key Properties:

  • \(0 \leq P(A) \leq 1\) for any event \(A\)
  • \(P(\text{certain event}) = 1\)
  • \(P(\text{impossible event}) = 0\)
  • \(P(\text{not A}) = 1 - P(A)\)
  • For mutually exclusive events \(A\) and \(B\):
    \[ P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Simple Probability Examples
slides.html:
Example 1: A fair die is rolled. What is the probability of getting an even number?

\[ \text{Even numbers: } \{2, 4, 6\} \Rightarrow \text{Favorable outcomes: } 3 \]
\[ \text{Total outcomes: } 6 \Rightarrow P(\text{even}) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2} \]

Example 2: A card is drawn from a standard deck. What is the probability of drawing a heart?

\[ \text{Hearts: } 13,\quad \text{Total cards: } 52 \Rightarrow P(\text{heart}) = \frac{13}{52} = \frac{1}{4} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Conditional Probability (Concept)
slides.html:
Conditional Probability is the probability of event \(A\) occurring given that event \(B\) has occurred.

\[ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \]

Key Points:

  • \(P(A \mid B)\) narrows the sample space to only outcomes where \(B\) happens.
  • Only defined if \(P(B) \ne 0\).

Visual:

Think of "restricting" the universe to \(B\), and checking how many of those are also \(A\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Probability - Score Distribution Table
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The same 20 contestants, on each of 3 days, answered 5 questions in order to win a prize. Each contestant received 1 point for each correct answer. The number of contestants receiving a given score on each day is shown in the table below.

ScoreDay 1Day 2Day 3Total
5 out of 52237
4 out of 53339
3 out of 545313
2 out of 565415
1 out of 52439
0 out of 53126
Total20202060

No contestant received the same score on two different days. If a contestant is selected at random, what is the probability that the selected contestant received a score of 5 on Day 2 or Day 3, given that the contestant received a score of 5 on one of the three days?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Probability - Score Distribution Table
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Problem Context:

  • 20 contestants each day, over 3 days (no repeat scores).
  • Table shows number of contestants scoring 0–5 on each day.
  • Total scores of 5 across all days: \(2 + 2 + 3 = 7\)

Given: A contestant received a score of \(5\) on one of the days.

Find: Probability that this contestant scored \(5\) on Day 2 or Day 3.

Step 1: Count favorable outcomes

\[ \text{Day 2: } 2 \qquad \text{Day 3: } 3 \qquad \text{Total favorable} = 2 + 3 = 5 \]

Step 2: Count total outcomes

\[ \text{Total contestants with score of 5: } 7 \]
Answer
\[ P(\text{Day 2 or Day 3} \mid \text{Score 5}) = \frac{5}{7} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{\frac{5}{7}}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

The table summarizes the distribution of age and favorite sport for 150 members of a country club.

SquashTennisGolfTotal
30–44 years18102250
45–59 years11142550
60+ years2126350
Total505050150

One of these members will be selected at random. What is the probability of selecting a member whose favorite sport is tennis, given that the member is at least 45 years of age? (Express your answer as a decimal or fraction, not as a percent.)

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: The table summarizes the distribution of age and…
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Identify the relevant group:

\[ \text{At least 45 years of age} = (45–59) + (60+) = 50 + 50 = 100. \]

Step 2. Find the total number of tennis players in that group:

\[ 14 (\text{45–59}) + 26 (\text{60+}) = 40. \]

Step 3. Calculate the probability:

\[ P(\text{Tennis} \mid \text{At least 45}) = \frac{40}{100} = 0.4. \]
Answer
\(\boxed{0.4}\).\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A new medical test is designed to give a positive indicator to a patient who has a certain virus and a negative indicator to a patient who does not. The table summarizes the results for 1,000 patients who have taken this test.

Negative IndicatorPositive IndicatorTotal
Has virus30370400
Does not have virus55050600
Total5804201,000

Based on the table, what is the probability that the test gives the wrong indicator?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: A new medical test is designed to give a positive…
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Count the number of wrong indicators:

  • False negatives (Has virus, but test negative): 30
  • False positives (Does not have virus, but test positive): 50
\[ 30 + 50 = 80 \]

Step 2. Divide by the total number of patients:

\[ \frac{80}{1,000} = 0.08 \]

Step 3. Answer:

\[ 0.08 = 8\% \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

PresenterJack ZengFromNovel Prep

Tap a section above, or press Next for the full outline

slides.title: Lesson outline
slides.html:
Lesson outline

SAT Unit 3.6

Inference and Margin of Error

25 slides · tap any section to jump there instantly.
slides.study_tip: Use this outline to jump straight to any section — great for review or picking up where you left off.

slides.title: Up next Inference from Sample Statistics
slides.html:
Up next

Inference from Sample Statistics
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 1 — What Is Statistical Inference?
slides.html:
Inference = using data from a sample to draw conclusions about a population.

Why use a sample?

  • Studying an entire population is often impractical.
  • A random sample can give a reliable estimate.

Important: Conclusions are estimates, not certainties.

Example: If 65% of 200 randomly selected voters support a policy, we estimate that about 65% of the population supports it.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 2 — Parameter vs.\ Statistic
slides.html:
Population (unknown)Sample (known)
NameParameterStatistic
Mean\(\mu\)\(\bar{x}\)
Proportion\(p\)\(\hat{p}\)

On the SAT:

  • Use the sample proportion to predict the population.
  • Scale up: \(\text{population count} \approx \text{sample proportion} \times \text{total population}\).
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 3 — Using a Poll to Predict an Election
slides.html:
Three-step method (very common on the SAT):

  1. Find each proportion in the sample.
  2. Multiply each proportion by the total number of voters.
  3. Subtract to find the expected margin of victory.

Example setup: Poll of 803 voters \(\Rightarrow\) apply those proportions to 6,424 actual voters.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Predicting Election Outcome from Poll Data
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The table shows the results of a poll. A total of \( 803 \) voters selected at random were asked which candidate they would vote for in the upcoming election.

CandidateVotes
Angel Cruz483
Terry Smith320

According to the poll, if \( 6,424 \) people vote in the election, by how many votes would Angel Cruz be expected to win?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Predicting Election Outcome from Poll Data
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Find proportions from the poll:

\[ \frac{483}{803} \text{ for Cruz}, \qquad \frac{320}{803} \text{ for Smith}. \]

Step 2. Scale to 6,424 voters:

\[ \text{Cruz: } \frac{483}{803} \times 6424 \approx 3864, \qquad \text{Smith: } \frac{320}{803} \times 6424 \approx 2560. \]

Step 3. Find the margin:

\[ 3864 - 2560 = 1304. \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Margin of Error
slides.html:
01Up next

Margin of Error

slides.title: Step 4 — What Margin of Error Means
slides.html:
Margin of error (MOE) = how far the sample estimate might be from the true population value.

If the estimate is \(30\%\) with MOE \(3\%\), the plausible range is:

\[ 30\% \pm 3\% = [27\%,\ 33\%] \]

Plain-language guide for the SAT:

  • Inside the interval: plausible / reasonable / could be true
  • Outside the interval: doubtful / unlikely (but usually not ``impossible'')
  • MOE is not about misclassified individuals in the sample
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 5 — What Changes Margin of Error?
slides.html:
Factors that affect MOE:

  • Sample size (\(n\)): Larger \(n\) \(\Rightarrow\) smaller MOE

    SAT shortcut: If two samples use the same method and one MOE is bigger, the bigger MOE usually means a smaller sample size.

    Reference formula (proportion):

    \[ \text{MOE} = z^* \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Interpreting Margin of Error
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In a study of cell phone use, 799 randomly selected US teens were asked how often they talked on a cell phone and about their texting behavior. The data are summarized in the table below.

Texting behaviorTalks on cell phone dailyDoes not talk on cell phone daily
Light110146
Medium139164
Heavy16674
Total415384

Based on the data from the study, an estimate of the percent of US teens who are heavy texters is \( 30\% \), and the associated margin of error is \( 3\% \). Which of the following is a correct statement based on the given margin of error?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Interpreting Margin of Error
slides.html:
The confidence interval is \(30\% \pm 3\% = [27\%,\ 33\%]\).

  • A is wrong: MOE does not measure misclassification in the sample.
  • B is wrong: values below 27% are less likely, not impossible.
  • C is wrong: 33% is only the upper bound, not the exact value.
  • D is correct: 35% is outside the interval, so it is doubtful.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Margin of Error and Sample Size
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The table below shows the results of two random samples of votes for a proposition.

SamplePercent in favorMargin of error
A52%4.2%
B48%1.6%

The samples were selected from the same population, and the margins of error were calculated using the same method. Which of the following is the most appropriate reason that the margin of error for sample A is greater than the margin of error for sample B?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Margin of Error and Sample Size
slides.html:
Margin of error decreases as sample size increases. Since both samples used the same method, the larger MOE for Sample A means it had a smaller sample size.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Practice
slides.html:
02Up next

Practice

slides.title: Question: Gymnasts Under Pressure Study
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In a psychological study, 600 college gymnasts were randomly selected and put in high-stakes competitions to determine whether they performed better under pressure. Based on the results of this study, it is estimated that 65% of all college gymnasts perform better under pressure, with an associated margin of error of 4%. Which of the following inferences can appropriately be drawn based on the given margin of error?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Gymnasts Under Pressure Study
slides.html:
The interval is \(65\% \pm 4\% = [61\%,\ 69\%]\).

  • A is wrong: the interval includes 61%, not just 65% to 69%.
  • B is wrong: MOE is not about individual misclassification.
  • C is correct: 60% is below 61%, so it is unlikely.
  • D is wrong: 70% is outside the interval but not strictly impossible.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Governor Election Vote Margin
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Two candidates are running for governor of a state. A recent poll reports that out of a random sample of 250 voters, 110 support candidate A and 140 support candidate B. An estimated 500,000 state residents are expected to vote on election day. According to the poll, candidate B is expected to receive how many more votes than candidate A?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Governor Election Vote Margin
slides.html:
Walk through the solution

Step 1. Sample proportions: \(110/250 = 0.44\) for A, \(140/250 = 0.56\) for B.

Step 2. Expected votes: \(500,000 \times 0.44 = 220,000\) and \(500,000 \times 0.56 = 280,000\).

Step 3. Difference: \(280,000 - 220,000 = 60,000\).

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Town Survey Response Bias
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

All the residents of a town were mailed a survey about proposed renovations to the town's high school. Approximately 10% of the residents completed the survey and mailed it back. Based on the responses, it was estimated that 72% of the residents support the proposed renovations, with an associated margin of error of 12%. Which of the following best explains why these survey results are unlikely to represent the opinions of all the town's residents?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Town Survey Response Bias
slides.html:
Only about 10% responded, and those who chose to respond may have stronger opinions. This is voluntary response bias, so the sample is not representative of all residents.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Pet Store Sample Size and MOE
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

For a study, a pet supply store selected two random samples of its customers. Based on data gathered from each sample, the percentage of customers who own a cat was estimated. The results are shown in the table.

SamplePercentage of customers who own a catMargin of error
A27%4%
B30%6%

If the margin of error was calculated the same way for both samples, which of the following is the most likely reason that the margin of error for sample B is greater than the margin of error for sample A?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Pet Store Sample Size and MOE
slides.html:
A larger margin of error usually means a smaller sample size. Sample B's larger MOE most likely means it included fewer customers.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In a study of cell phone use, 799 randomly selected US teens were asked how often they talked on a cell phone and about their texting behavior. The data are summarized in the table below.

Based on the data from the study, an estimate of the percent of US teens who are heavy texters is \( 30\% \), and the associated margin of error is \( 3\% \). Which of the following is a correct statement based on the given margin of error?

choices.text: Approximately \( 3\% \) of the teens in the study who are classified as heavy texters are not really heavy texters.

The percent of all US teens who are heavy texters is \( 33\% \).

checkpoint.title: Margin of Error and Sample Size
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

The table below shows the results of two random samples of votes for a proposition.

SamplePercent in favorMargin of error
A52%4.2%
B48%1.6%

The samples were selected from the same population, and the margins of error were calculated using the same method. Which of the following is the most appropriate reason that the margin of error for sample A is greater than the margin of error for sample B?

choices.text: Sample A had a smaller number of votes that could not be recorded.
Sample A had a higher percent of favorable responses.
Sample A had a larger sample size.
Sample A had a smaller sample size.

checkpoint.title: Gymnasts Under Pressure Study
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

In a psychological study, 600 college gymnasts were randomly selected and put in high-stakes competitions to determine whether they performed better under pressure. Based on the results of this study, it is estimated that 65% of all college gymnasts perform better under pressure, with an associated margin of error of 4%. Which of the following inferences can appropriately be drawn based on the given margin of error?

choices.text: The percentage of all college gymnasts who perform better under pressure is between 65% and 69%.
Approximately 4% of the college gymnasts in the study who performed better under pressure do not actually perform better under pressure.
It is unlikely that the percentage of all college gymnasts who perform better under pressure is 60%.
It is not possible that the percentage of all college gymnasts who perform better under pressure is 70%.

checkpoint.title: Governor Election Vote Margin
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Two candidates are running for governor of a state. A recent poll reports that out of a random sample of 250 voters, 110 support candidate A and 140 support candidate B. An estimated 500,000 state residents are expected to vote on election day. According to the poll, candidate B is expected to receive how many more votes than candidate A?

checkpoint.title: Town Survey Response Bias
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

All the residents of a town were mailed a survey about proposed renovations to the town's high school. Approximately 10% of the residents completed the survey and mailed it back. Based on the responses, it was estimated that 72% of the residents support the proposed renovations, with an associated margin of error of 12%. Which of the following best explains why these survey results are unlikely to represent the opinions of all the town's residents?

choices.text: The survey included residents who do not attend the high school.
Not all residents were aware of the proposed renovations before the survey.
Responses to the survey were not obtained from a random sample of the town's residents.
The margin of error is greater than the percentage of residents who responded to the survey.

checkpoint.title: Pet Store Sample Size and MOE
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

For a study, a pet supply store selected two random samples of its customers. Based on data gathered from each sample, the percentage of customers who own a cat was estimated. The results are shown in the table.

SamplePercentage of customers who own a catMargin of error
A27%4%
B30%6%

If the margin of error was calculated the same way for both samples, which of the following is the most likely reason that the margin of error for sample B is greater than the margin of error for sample A?

choices.text: Sample B included a greater number of customers who own a cat than sample A.
Sample B included a greater percentage of customers who own a cat than sample A.
Sample B included more customers than sample A.
Sample B included fewer customers than sample A.

slides.title: Lesson outline
slides.html:
Lesson outline

SAT Unit 3.7

Evaluating Statistical Claims

25 slides · tap any section to jump there instantly.
slides.study_tip: Use this outline to jump straight to any section — great for review or picking up where you left off.

slides.title: Up next Observation Study and Experiments
slides.html:
Up next

Observation Study and Experiments
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 1 — Population and Sample
slides.html:
Population: The entire group you want information about.

Sample: A subset of the population used to make inferences.

Goal: Use data from a sample to draw conclusions about the population.

  • Random sample: Every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected.
  • Representative sample: The sample reflects the population you care about.
  • Larger sample: Usually gives a more reliable estimate.

Caution: A biased or non-random sample can make conclusions invalid.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 2 — Statistical Generalization (SAT Core)
slides.html:
Question the SAT asks: ``To which group can we apply these results?''

Golden rule:

  • You can generalize to the population that was randomly sampled.
  • You cannot generalize to a larger group that was not sampled.

Example: A random sample of 40 fourth-graders at one school \(\Rightarrow\) results apply to all fourth-graders at that school — not the whole county or all students.

SAT shortcut: Pick the largest population that still matches the sampling group.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 3 — Three Types of Studies
slides.html:
|p{4.2cm}|p{4.2cm}|} \hline TypeWhat happens?Can show causation?
Sample surveyAsk questions; no treatment assignedNo
Observational studyObserve subjects; researcher does not impose a treatmentNo — association only
ExperimentResearcher assigns a treatment and compares outcomesYes — if well designed

Key SAT distinction:

  • Observational: ``We watched what happened.''
  • Experiment: ``We assigned groups and compared results.''
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 4 — Experiments vs.\ Observational Studies
slides.html:
Observational study:

  • Researchers observe outcomes without assigning treatments.
  • Can identify association, not causation.

Experiment:

  • Researchers impose a treatment and measure its effect.
  • Can support causation if random assignment and a control group are used.

SAT alert: If there is no random assignment, do not claim the treatment caused the outcome.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Step 5 — Bias and Invalid Conclusions
slides.html:
Bias: A systematic error that makes results misleading.

Common types on the SAT:

  • Sampling bias: Sample does not represent the population (e.g., survey only at a playground).
  • Voluntary response bias: People choose whether to respond (e.g., online poll after a TV show).
  • Nonresponse bias: Many selected people do not respond.
  • Question wording bias: Leading or confusing questions.

If the sample is biased, the conclusion is not reliable — even if the math is correct.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: SAT Strategy — Evaluating a Statistical Claim
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Use this checklist on every question:

  1. Who was sampled? (Identify the exact group.)
  2. Was the sample random? (If not, generalization fails.)
  3. Is the sample biased? (Location, voluntary response, etc.)
  4. Survey, observational study, or experiment? (Causation only in experiments.)
  5. Largest valid population? (Match the sampled group — no bigger.)

Teaching tip: Students often pick an answer that sounds ``bigger'' or ``more general.'' On the SAT, valid beats broad.

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Question: Statistical Generalization
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A sample of 40 fourth-grade students was selected at random from a certain school.

The 40 students completed a survey about the morning announcements, and 32 thought the announcements were helpful.

Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of the survey can be applied?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Statistical Generalization
slides.html:
The 40 students were selected at random from fourth-grade students at the school. So the results can be generalized to that group — but not to all students at the school or to the whole county.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Interpreting Poll Validity
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Near the end of a US cable news show, the host invited viewers to respond to a poll on the show's website that asked, ``Do you support the new federal policy discussed during the show?''

At the end of the show, the host reported that \(28\%\) responded ``Yes,'' and \(70\%\) responded ``No.''

Which of the following best explains why the results are unlikely to represent the sentiments of the population of the United States?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Interpreting Poll Validity
slides.html:
Viewers who chose to respond are a voluntary, non-random sample. They are not representative of all US residents, so the poll cannot reliably estimate national opinion.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Survey Sampling and Bias
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

To determine the mean number of children per household in a community, Tabitha surveyed 20 families at a playground. For the 20 families surveyed, the mean number of children per household was \( 2.4 \).

Which of the following statements must be true?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Survey Sampling and Bias
slides.html:
Surveying only families at a playground introduces sampling bias: these families are more likely to have children and may not represent all households in the community.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Chess Memorization Study Generalization
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A researcher conducted a study to determine if certain memorization strategies could improve the performance of chess players. For the study, the researcher selected a random sample of adults who are competing in a chess tournament. At the end of the study, 80% of those sampled reported that they did not find the memorization strategies to be useful during their chess games. Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of this study can be applied?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Chess Memorization Study Generalization
slides.html:
The sample was drawn from adults competing in a chess tournament. The largest valid generalization matches that exact population.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Teacher Certification Survey
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A group of school administrators wanted to gather opinions on new teacher certification requirements that were introduced in a certain state. The group surveyed a random sample of math teachers from one of the school districts in the state. The survey showed that the majority of the teachers in the sample support the new requirements. Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of the survey can be generalized?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Teacher Certification Survey
slides.html:
The random sample came from math teachers in one school district. Generalizing to the whole state or to all teachers in the district would go beyond the sampled population.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Library vs.\ Home Study Experiment
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A researcher wanted to determine whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home. He selected a random sample of 200 students and gave them three days to study for the same test. The 100 students who lived closest to a library were asked to study at a library, and the remaining 100 students were asked to study at home. After the students took the test, the scores of those who studied at a library were compared to the scores of those who studied at home. How should this experiment be changed so that the researcher can conclude whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Library vs.\ Home Study Experiment
slides.html:
Assignment by proximity to a library is not random and may confound the results (e.g., location, schedule, or background differences). Random assignment to study location isolates the effect of where students study.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Company Social Events Survey
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A company consists of full-time and part-time employees. A sample of 60 employees was selected at random from the company and given a survey to determine how many social events the company should have each year. There are 59 part-time employees and 1 full-time employee in the sample. Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of the survey can be generalized?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Company Social Events Survey
slides.html:
The sample was selected at random from all employees at the company (both full-time and part-time). Even though the sample happened to include mostly part-time workers, the valid generalization is still the full employee population.

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{B}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

checkpoint.title: Interpreting Poll Validity
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Near the end of a US cable news show, the host invited viewers to respond to a poll on the show's website that asked, ``Do you support the new federal policy discussed during the show?''

At the end of the show, the host reported that \(28\%\) responded ``Yes,'' and \(70\%\) responded ``No.''

Which of the following best explains why the results are unlikely to represent the sentiments of the population of the United States?

choices.text: The percentages do not add up to \(100\%\), so any possible conclusions from the poll are invalid.
Those who responded to the poll were not a random sample of the population of the United States.
There were not \(50\%\) ``Yes'' responses and \(50\%\) ``No'' responses.
The show did not allow viewers enough time to respond to the poll.

checkpoint.title: Survey Sampling and Bias
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

To determine the mean number of children per household in a community, Tabitha surveyed 20 families at a playground. For the 20 families surveyed, the mean number of children per household was \( 2.4 \).

Which of the following statements must be true?

choices.text: The mean number of children per household in the community is \( 2.4 \).
A determination about the mean number of children per household in the community should not be made because the sample size is too small.
The sampling method is flawed and may produce a biased estimate of the mean number of children per household in the community.
The sampling method is not flawed and is likely to produce an unbiased estimate of the mean number of children per household in the community.

Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of this study can be applied?

All adults who compete in chess tournaments.
All chess players competing in the tournament.

checkpoint.title: Library vs.\ Home Study Experiment
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

A researcher wanted to determine whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home. He selected a random sample of 200 students and gave them three days to study for the same test. After the students took the test, the scores of those who studied at a library were compared to the scores of those who studied at home. How should this experiment be changed so that the researcher can conclude whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home?

choices.text: The students assigned to study at a library should study at the same library.
